mainmatter.p: Let \mathbb {T} be a geometric theory and let U_{ \mathbb {T} } be its generic model in \mathbf {Set} { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathbb {T} \right ] \mathclose {}}. The spectrum of a U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra M is the set of U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra homomorphisms M \to U_{ \mathbb {T} }. (Recall that a U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra is a model of \mathbb {T} equipped with a homomorphism from U_{ \mathbb {T} }.)

Let \mathbb {T} be a horn theory. The generic \mathbb {T}-model U_{ \mathbb {T} } is quasicoherent in the following sense: internal to \mathbf {Set} { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathbb {T} \right ] \mathclose {}}, for any finitely presentable U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra A, the canonical U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra homomorphism A \to U_{ \mathbb {T} } ^{ \operatorname {Spec} {A}} sending a to \lambda f: \operatorname {Spec} {A}. f \, a is an isomorphism, where \operatorname {Spec} {A} is the synthetic spectrum of A.

frontmatter.title: Synthetic quasicoherence in the object classifier

mainmatter.p: Let V be the generic object in the object classifier \mathbf {Set} { \mathopen {} \left [ V \right ] \mathclose {}}. Then the map V+1 \to V^V mapping \mathsf { inl } \, x to \lambda \_ .x and \mathsf { inr } \, { \mathopen {} \left ( \right ) \mathclose {}} to \lambda x.x is an isomorphism.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We consider the finitely presentable V-algebra V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}}, i.e. the free extension of V by an element \mathsf { x }. By synthetic quasicoherence, the canonical homomorphism V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} \to V^{ \operatorname {Spec} { V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} }} is an isomorphism. We will see that this homomorphism is, in fact, tracked (up to isomorphism) by the desired map V+1 \to V^V.

First of all, the carrier of V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} is the coproduct V+1. The spectrum of V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} can be seen to be V itself by the universal property of the free extension:
block.tex: \operatorname {Spec} V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} = \mathbf {hom} _{ \mathrm {Alg}_{V} } { \mathopen {} \left ( V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} , V \right ) \mathclose {}} \cong V
block.p: The canonical isomorphism above sends a homomorphism { \operatorname {Spec} { V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} } } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ V } to the element f \mathsf { x }. We therefore have the desired isomorphism V+1 \cong V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} \cong V^{ \operatorname {Spec} { V { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathsf { x } \right ] \mathclose {}} }} \cong V^V.

frontmatter.title: A general Nullstellensatz for generalized spaces

mainmatter.p: We give a general Nullstellensatz for the generic model of a geometric theory, useful as a source of nongeometric sequents validated by the generic model, and characterize the first-order and higher-order formulas validated by the generic model.
